name: Windows Operating System Features
description: Windows editions, features, system utilities, and administrative tools
key_facts: Windows 10/11 editions: Home, Pro, Pro for Workstations, Enterprise
Pro and Enterprise editions support BitLocker, Group Policy, Remote Desktop hosting, and Hyper-V
Task Manager shows running processes, CPU/memory usage, startup programs, and services
System Configuration (msconfig) manages boot options and startup services
Windows uses NTFS as the default file system, supporting permissions, encryption, and large files
common_misconceptions: Windows Home supports BitLocker full-disk encryption (only Pro and Enterprise do)
All Windows editions support Group Policy Editor (Home edition does not include gpedit.msc)

name: Command-Line Tools
description: Windows command prompt, PowerShell, and Linux/macOS terminal commands for system management
key_facts: Windows commands: ipconfig, sfc /scannow, chkdsk, gpupdate, shutdown, robocopy
PowerShell uses cmdlets with Verb-Noun syntax (Get-Process, Set-ExecutionPolicy)
Linux commands: ls, chmod, chown, grep, ps, top, df, cat, nano, vi
File permissions in Linux use rwx notation: read (4), write (2), execute (1)
sfc /scannow checks and repairs Windows system file integrity
common_misconceptions: Command Prompt and PowerShell are the same (PowerShell is more powerful with object-oriented pipelines)
chmod 777 is a good practice (it gives everyone full access, which is a security risk)

name: macOS and Linux Basics
description: Key features and tools of macOS and Linux operating systems relevant to IT support
key_facts: macOS uses APFS (Apple File System) as the default file system
Time Machine is the built-in macOS backup utility
Linux distributions include Ubuntu, Fedora, CentOS, and Debian
Linux uses ext4 as the most common file system
apt (Debian/Ubuntu) and yum/dnf (RHEL/Fedora) are package managers
common_misconceptions: Linux is a single operating system (Linux is a kernel; distributions bundle it with different software)
macOS cannot run in enterprise environments (macOS is supported with MDM tools like Jamf)

name: Malware Types and Removal
description: Common types of malware, their behaviors, and removal procedures
key_facts: Virus attaches to a file and requires user action to execute and spread
Worm self-replicates across networks without user interaction
Trojan disguises itself as legitimate software to trick users into installing it
Ransomware encrypts user files and demands payment for the decryption key
Malware removal steps: Identify symptoms, Quarantine system, Disable System Restore, Remediate, Schedule scans, Enable System Restore, Educate user
common_misconceptions: Antivirus software catches all malware (zero-day threats and polymorphic malware can evade detection)
Macs cannot get malware (macOS is less targeted but not immune to malware)

name: Social Engineering and Physical Security
description: Social engineering attack types and physical security measures for IT environments
key_facts: Phishing uses fraudulent emails to trick users into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links
Spear phishing targets specific individuals or organizations with personalized messages
Tailgating occurs when an unauthorized person follows an authorized person through a secured door
Shoulder surfing is observing someone entering sensitive information by looking over their shoulder
Physical security includes mantraps, biometric locks, security cameras, and cable locks
common_misconceptions: Social engineering only happens online (physical techniques like tailgating and dumpster diving are also common)
Only non-technical users fall for social engineering (even IT professionals can be targeted successfully)

name: Authentication and Access Control
description: Authentication methods, MFA, and access control models for securing systems
key_facts: MFA uses two or more factors: something you know, something you have, something you are
NTFS permissions control file and folder access on Windows systems
UAC (User Account Control) prompts users before making system changes
Principle of least privilege grants users only the minimum access needed
Mobile device security includes screen locks, remote wipe, and full device encryption
common_misconceptions: A password plus a security question is MFA (both are something you know, so it is single-factor)
Administrator accounts should be used for daily work (standard accounts with UAC are more secure)

name: Windows Troubleshooting
description: Diagnosing and resolving common Windows OS issues including boot problems, crashes, and performance
key_facts: Safe Mode loads Windows with minimal drivers for troubleshooting
Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) provides repair tools when the OS will not boot
System Restore reverts system files and settings to a previous restore point without affecting user data
Event Viewer logs system, application, and security events for diagnostic analysis
Startup Repair in WinRE automatically fixes common boot issues
common_misconceptions: System Restore recovers deleted user files (it only restores system settings and programs)
A factory reset is the only fix for a non-booting Windows (Startup Repair and bootrec can often resolve boot issues)

name: Application and Browser Troubleshooting
description: Resolving application crashes, browser issues, and software compatibility problems
key_facts: Application crashes can result from insufficient memory, corrupted files, or compatibility issues
Compatibility Mode runs applications as if they were on an older Windows version
Browser issues include certificate errors, plugin failures, and redirect loops
Clearing browser cache and cookies resolves many website loading issues
Application logs in Event Viewer help identify the root cause of crashes
common_misconceptions: Reinstalling always fixes application issues (sometimes the issue is with system dependencies or configuration)
Browser extensions cannot cause security issues (malicious extensions can steal data or inject ads)

name: Backup and Recovery Procedures
description: Backup types, strategies, and disaster recovery planning for data protection
key_facts: Full backup copies all selected data; takes longest but simplest to restore
Incremental backup copies only data changed since the last backup (full or incremental)
Differential backup copies data changed since the last full backup
The 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 offsite copy
Cloud backup provides offsite storage with automatic versioning
common_misconceptions: Differential and incremental backups are the same (differential backs up since last full; incremental since last any backup)
RAID eliminates the need for backups (RAID protects against drive failure, not data corruption or deletion)

name: Documentation and Change Management
description: IT documentation standards, change management processes, and professional best practices
key_facts: Change management follows a process: request, review, approve, implement, document
Ticketing systems track issues from creation to resolution
Knowledge base articles document common problems and their solutions
Network documentation includes topology diagrams, IP address schemes, and asset inventory
Acceptable Use Policies (AUPs) define how employees may use company IT resources
common_misconceptions: Documentation is optional for small IT teams (documentation prevents knowledge loss and improves consistency)
Change management slows down IT operations (it prevents unplanned outages from untested changes)

name: Safety and Environmental Procedures
description: Electrical safety, ESD prevention, proper disposal of equipment, and environmental considerations
key_facts: ESD (electrostatic discharge) can damage components; use an anti-static wrist strap when handling
CRT monitors contain high-voltage capacitors and should be serviced by trained technicians
Batteries and toner cartridges must be disposed of according to local environmental regulations
MSDS/SDS (Safety Data Sheets) provide information about handling hazardous materials
Proper lifting technique: bend at the knees, keep back straight, lift with legs
common_misconceptions: ESD damage is always immediately obvious (latent ESD damage can cause intermittent failures later)
Household trash disposal is acceptable for electronics (e-waste must be recycled properly)
